name: Eddie Sauter
abstract: Edward Ernest Sauter (December 2, 1914 – April 21, 1981) was an American composer and arranger during the swing era.

about_quotes.speaker: Wayne Andre
about_quotes.evidence: I auditioned in the same studio... took an audition for the Sauter-Finegan band... I got the job. And I remember, I’ll never forget, Eddie Sauter coming around with a questionnaire to everybody before they got up to sit in and play with the band
about_quotes.doc_title: Wayne Andre interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Shiner
about_quotes.evidence: Eddie called me three days later and he says, 'We’d like to have you on the band,' I started to cry.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Shiner interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 11, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: Never got to know Eddie and I was always such an admirer of his, he was wonderful.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Percy Heath
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Eddie Sauter only in the context of a list of composers/works (Stravinsky, Milhaud) present at a festival where the Modern Jazz Quartet performed. There is no indication of a personal or professional relationship between Percy Heath and Eddie Sauter.
about_quotes.doc_title: Percy Heath — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: James Chirillo
about_quotes.evidence: Eddie Sauter studied with him too... Eddie Sauter wrote for Benny Goodman... Eddie Sauter went up to Béla Bartok and introduced himself
about_quotes.doc_title: James Chirillo interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: We split. Incidentally, that band, the trumpet section's rather interesting. It was Chris Griffin, Eddie Sauter, and Tootie Camarata.
